headline: Boston College's Brennan erupts in blowout; Michigan's offense stalls early
body: Duke Brennan caught eight passes for 129 yards and three touchdowns to lead Boston College to a 36-9 rout of Michigan on Saturday. Brennan's performance—especially his ability to work underneath and turn catches into separation—gave the Eagles' passing game a reliable outlet that Michigan's defense simply could not contain. Quarterback Leavitt spread the ball effectively across the field, completing 20 of 33 attempts for 259 yards, but Brennan was the clear focal point of the Eagles' offensive gameplan.

Michigan's offense never found rhythm. Gabriel managed 244 passing yards on 21 completions but the efficiency wasn't there when it mattered most. The Wolverines' run game produced just 78 yards on the ground, forcing Gabriel to beat Boston College through the air—a matchup the Eagles' secondary eventually won. A turnover in Michigan territory late in the first half essentially ended any comeback hopes, as Boston College's defense tightened and the Eagles' scoring machine continued to hum.

Boston College's defensive unit, led by Cunningham's five tackles, limited Michigan's explosive plays and forced uncomfortable timing decisions. The Eagles allowed just 318 total yards and never yielded more than field goals in the first half, establishing control early and never relinquishing it. This was a complete performance by a team firing on multiple levels.
turningPoint: Brennan's first-half touchdown reception on third-and-long gave Boston College a two-score lead that Michigan could not close, shifting momentum decisively in the Eagles' favor.

unsungHero.name: Cunningham
unsungHero.why: While Brennan's receiving clinic grabbed the spotlight, Cunningham's five tackles helped set the tone for Boston College's suffocating defensive performance that kept Michigan off the field and limited explosive plays throughout.
